summary: AI-powered coding tools are fundamentally reshaping the traditional 'build vs. buy' decision in software, allowing individuals to rapidly prototype and build applications that previously required purchasing from vendors. While current AI isn't ready for full enterprise-grade development, the exponential pace of improvement means software vendors face an existential 'moat problem' as AI increasingly replicates the specific features users actually need.
key_points: AI coding tools enable rapid prototyping that shrinks learning cycles from months to minutes
Software vendors don't need to be fully replicated to lose business—AI just needs to build the specific functionality users need
The role of engineers is shifting from 'builder' to 'orchestrator'
Traditional vendor moats (features, implementation quality) are eroding; only proprietary data, network effects, and deep relationships will remain defensible
Despite valid concerns about hallucinations and reliability, AI labs are actively solving these problems
money_quotes: Why would I buy software when I can use AI to build it in 20 minutes?
The question has changed from 'build versus buy' to 'AI-generated versus buy.' This is a huge deal that people are only now beginning to understand.
AI doesn't need to create a wholesale replacement. It just needs to build the functionality a person or company needs.
Your role is evolving from 'builder' to 'orchestrator.' The questions change from 'How do we build this?' to 'What should we build, and can AI handle it?'
The ROI calculations look entirely different when development time shrinks from months to minutes, and the cost of code drops to near zero.
key_insight: The competitive moat for software vendors is rapidly eroding not because AI will wholesale replace their products, but because it can increasingly replicate just the specific edges of functionality that users actually need.
